content.full_text:
Telephone Call from Tony Kline 10-20-75


TO:        File

FROM:      Ken Finney

SUBJECT:   Potential Problems with Hiring a Partner in a Law
           Firm that Is A Lobbyist Employer as a Special
           Consultant


Mr. Kline asked a series of questions.  First, would the
partner who is hired as a part-time consultant to the Governor
be a public official?  Second, would the fact that the law
firm in which the individual is a partner employs lobbyists on
behalf of one of its clients create any special problems?

I indicated to Mr. Kline that the definition of "public
official" under Chapter 7 of the Act is still being debated
by the Commission but that I thought someone hired as a con-
sultant might be included in the eventual definition adopted
by the Commission; and I further stated that, if he was included
within the definition, he would be subject to the prohibition
contained in Section 87100.

On the second question, I indicated that the fact that the
individual being hired was a partner in a law firm which employs
a lobbyist would not preclude the Governor from hiring him.  It
would, however, probably subject the law firm (and the client
for whom the lobbyist is employed) to a reporting obligation
under Section 86109 of the Act since compensation paid the
partner employed as a consultant would be an exchange with an
agency official.


KF:gp

State of California

Memorandum

To : Phone Memo                                    Date : December 17, 1975


From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       TED PRIM

Subject: December 9, 1975, phone conversation with Yvonne Smith, Registrar
         in San Diego.

         Ms. Smith called me with the following question:

         A committee organized under Waxman Dymally had surplus funds
         of $8,000 on January 6, 1975. Since January 6, the committee
         has not received any contributions nor made any expenditures.
         Ms. Smith asked if the committee were required to file a
         statement of organization pursuant to Government Code Section
         84101 which states "every committee which is a committee
         by virtue of Section 82013(a) shall file with the Secretary
         of State a statement of organization within ten days after
         it is formed as a committee. Each such committee in existence
         at the date of enactment of this chapter shall file a statement
         with the Secretary of State within thirty days after the
         effective date of this chapter" Emphasis Added.

         I told her that the committee in question need not file
         a statement of organization so long as it did not receive contributions
         in the amount of $500 or more during the 1975 calendar year.
         It is my understanding that committees must qualify anew each
         year. The reference in Section 84101 to committees in existence
         merely afforded operating committees a thirty day period to file
         a statement of organization rather than the ten day period prescribed
         for newly formed committees. This provision does not require
         that every committee holding surplus funds on January 7, 1975
         must necessarily file a statement of organization.
